Plot StudioUser Guide · v2.9.0

Plot Studio User Guide

Beautiful statistical graphics for jamovi. Drop in a variable or two and get a polished chart with zero setup, then click anything on the chart to make it yours.
7 chart analyses + a chooser wizard Click-to-edit everything Honest statistics built in Colorblind-safe by default

Welcome

What is Plot Studio?

Plot Studio is a jamovi module that lives in the ribbon's Plots tab. It is organized around seven chart analyses and one guide.

AnalysisWhat it is for
DistributionThe shape and spread of one numeric variable: histograms, density curves, box / violin / raincloud, Q-Q plots, ECDF.
FrequenciesCounts or percentages of a categorical variable: bar (side-by-side, stacked, 100%), pie, donut, Pareto, with chi-square tests.
Compare GroupsA numeric outcome across the levels of a categorical variable (different people in each group): bar, line, dot, box, violin, raincloud.
Repeated MeasuresThe same people measured on two or more occasions (wide data, one column per occasion), with within-subject error bars.
ScatterThe relationship between two numeric variables, with fit lines, confidence bands, data ellipses, marginals, and a heatmap mode for big samples.
Correlation MatrixAll the pairwise correlations among two or more numeric variables, as a heatmap, circles, numbers, or a mixed matrix.
Likert / SurveyA battery of rating-scale items that share one response scale: diverging stacked bars, 100% stacked bars, or item means with CIs.
Help Me Choose (under a "Guide" divider)Not a chart: a question-driven wizard that recommends which analysis and chart type fit your goal or your exact variables.

The three ideas behind it

  • Beautiful defaults. Drop in one or two variables and the chart is already presentable: an accessible 10-color palette designed to remain distinct across Plot Studio's color-vision simulations, clean axes, sensible error bars.
  • Everything is click-to-edit. The left-hand jamovi panel stays tiny on purpose. Colors, fonts, axes, legends, labels, spacing, ordering: you edit them by clicking the thing itself on the chart. A style panel opens right below the chart.
  • Honest statistics. Significance brackets compute real tests from your raw data, disclose their corrections, and refuse politely when a test does not apply. A built-in Check graph tab scans your chart for the classic misleading-graph pitfalls before you export it.

Installing

  1. In jamovi, click the Modules button (the large + at the top-right of the ribbon).
  2. Choose jamovi library, find Plot Studio, and click Install. (If you were given a .jmo file instead, use Sideload, the sideways arrow icon, and pick the file.)
  3. A Plot Studio menu appears in the ribbon's Plots tab. If it does not show up right away, restart jamovi.

Your first chart in 60 seconds

  1. Open an analysis. Plots tab → Plot Studio → e.g. Compare Groups.
  2. Drop in variables. Drag a categorical variable into X-Axis Variable and a numeric one into Y-Axis Variable. Optionally add a second categorical variable to Group By (optional) for colored, side-by-side groups. The chart draws immediately.
  3. Switch the look if you like. Use the chart-type button at the top-left of the chart (it shows the current type, e.g. "Bar") to try Line, Dot, Box, Violin, or Raincloud. Not sure which? Click the ? button and open the Which graph? tab.
  4. Click anything to style it. Click a bar to recolor it, the axis to change its range, a label to rename it. Cmd/Ctrl+Z undoes any edit.
  5. Export. Click the export button (the tray icon) and save as SVG, PDF, PNG, or JPG.
TipThe native options panel is deliberately minimal: variable boxes plus one short data tip. Everything else lives on the chart itself. Click the part of the chart you want to change, or use the toolbar's magnifying-glass Find a setting button (Cmd/Ctrl+F) when you know the idea but not where it lives.

Picking the right chart

Which analysis do I open?

Match the shape of your data:

Your dataOpenTypical question
One numeric columnDistribution"How are reaction times distributed?"
One categorical columnFrequencies"How many people chose each option?"
A numeric outcome + a categorical grouping (different people per group)Compare Groups"Does the average score differ between conditions?"
The same people measured 2+ times, one column per occasion (wide format)Repeated Measures"Did scores change from before to after?"
Two numeric variablesScatter"Does anxiety change as sleep changes?"
Two or more numeric variablesCorrelation Matrix"How do these measures relate?"
Several survey items sharing one response scaleLikert / Survey"What is the balance of agreement across my questionnaire items?"
Not sureHelp Me ChooseThe wizard will tell you.

Help Me Choose (the wizard)

Help Me Choose sits at the bottom of the Plot Studio menu under a small "Guide" divider. It is not a chart; it is an interactive guide with two routes:

  • Question route. Leave its variables box empty and answer the prompts, starting with "What do you want your graph to show?", with seven cards running from "Compare a measurement across groups" to "Summarize survey or rating-scale items". Each terminal answer is a card headed USE THE ANALYSIS <name> with a plain-language reason and a shortlist of fitting graph types, each with a little preview icon.
  • Data route. Drop the exact variables you plan to plot into the "Variables to plot (optional)" box. A YOUR VARIABLES panel classifies each one (continuous, nominal, ordinal, levels counted), then the wizard recommends a best-match analysis ("Why this fits:" written from your variables) plus an alternative ("Choose this instead if:").

The wizard is honest about limits. If no single chart can show every variable you dropped in, an orange "Heads up" banner says so, and each card names which of your variables fit and which to leave out. It also knows survey batteries: several items sharing one response scale route to Likert / Survey (even when they are numeric 0-10 ratings), unless their names look like time points (t1, t2, week1…), in which case Repeated Measures leads. With two numeric variables and 500+ rows it suggests starting with Scatter's Heatmap type.

Good to knowThe wizard cannot open an analysis for you; jamovi does not allow it. Its card tells you to "Open <Analysis> from the Plot Studio menu, then drop in your variables." The wizard also resets to its first screen whenever it re-runs; it is a one-shot guide, nothing in it needs saving.

Already in a chart? Use "Which graph?"

Every chart's ? button has a Which graph? tab listing the chart types available for your current analysis, each with a plain-language "best for" line. The current type is highlighted and carries an honest Trade-off: line; every other card has a Use this button that switches immediately, keeping your styling. An amber Suggestion for your data box appears when your actual data argues for a different type (for example, "Your groups have enough data (smallest n = 20) to SHOW the distribution: a box, violin, or raincloud would reveal spread that a bar hides.").

The seven chart analyses

Each section below covers: the data the analysis expects, its variable boxes, its on-chart controls, the chart types it draws, and its special features.

Compare Groups

Between-subjects · long data · one row per observation

Compare a numeric outcome across the levels of a categorical variable as bar, line, dot, box, violin, or raincloud charts, with mean or median summaries, error bars for means, and optional significance brackets.

Your data (long)
conditionscoresex
Ctrl9.8F
Ctrl11.1M
Drug114.3F
………
Variable boxTakesRequired?
X-Axis Variableone categorical variable (the groups along the bottom)Yes
Y-Axis Variableone numeric variable (the outcome)Yes
Group By (optional)one categorical variable → colored side-by-side seriesNo
Panels (optional)one categorical variable → one mini-chart per levelNo

On-chart controls: Bar, Line, and Dot can summarize with a Mean or Median. Mean summaries can show Standard Error, Standard Deviation, 95% Confidence Interval, 99% Confidence Interval, or no error bars; median summaries draw no SE / SD / CI bars. Box, Violin, and Raincloud show the distribution itself.

Bar

Compare a summary (mean or median) across a few groups. Quick magnitude comparison, but it hides the distribution.

Line

Show a trend across an ordered X axis (time, dose). Avoid for unordered categories.

Dot

Each group's summary as a single dot with an error bar. Read by position, so it does not need a zero baseline the way bars do.

Box

Each group's centre and spread: median, middle 50% box, whiskers, outliers.

Violin

The full distribution shape (fatter = more data). Best with larger samples.

Raincloud

Distribution, a small box, and every raw point at once. The most transparent option.

Special features

  • Significance brackets with real tests computed from your raw data (see Significance brackets), plus a full pairwise-comparison and ANOVA suite in the Σ Statistics panel.
  • Value labels and N labels on bars ("+" menu). N labels also work on box / violin / raincloud.
  • Individual data points and outlier markers as overlays ("+" menu).
  • Manual ordering: drag categories or series directly on the chart, or use the Order tab's compact up / down controls for an exact one-step move.
  • Panels: an assigned Panels variable draws one mini-chart per level, with a clickable heading strip on each.

Repeated Measures

Within-subjects · wide data · one column per occasion, one row per subject

Plot an outcome recorded on the same people across two or more occasions or conditions. Error bars use the Cousineau-Morey within-subject correction by default, so they reflect the consistency of each person's changes rather than overall between-person spread.

Your data (wide)
t1t2t3sex
10.212.513.9F
9.411.012.2M
…………

Variable boxes: different from the other modules. Repeated Measures uses the same factorial input as jamovi's own RM-ANOVA:

  • Repeated Measures Factors: type the name of each within-subjects factor and its level names (e.g. a factor "Time" with levels T1, T2, T3).
  • Repeated Measures Cells: a drop target appears for every combination of levels ("drag variable here"); drag one wide-format column into each cell.
  • Between Subject Factors: optional categorical variables that split the design into colored groups (like Compare Groups' "Group By").

On-chart controls: Summary (Mean / Median), plus an Error bars tab whose Type control contains SE / SD / 95% CI / 99% CI / None. On Repeated Measures, that same Type surface includes a Method subsection: "Within-subjects (Cousineau-Morey)" (default) or "Between-subjects (uncorrected)". Median summaries draw no SE / SD / CI bars.

Arranging a factorial design. With two or more factors, a small strip under the toolbar offers three dropdowns (X-axis, Grouped by, Panels) so you can pivot any factor onto any slot instantly.

Line (default)

Joins each occasion so you can read the trend across time or conditions.

Bar

A summary per occasion with a within-subject error bar.

Dot

A dot at each occasion's summary, no connecting line.

Crossed factors

Two within-subjects factors share one chart: one on the X axis, one as colored series.

Special features

  • Per-subject overlays ("+" menu): Data points shows every subject's raw values; Connect subjects draws each person's own line across occasions ("spaghetti"), so you can see whether individuals follow the average trend.
  • Paired and mixed tests by design. Significance brackets between occasions default to a paired t-test, joined subject-by-subject. The Statistics panel's Omnibus tab reports repeated-measures effects for within-subject designs and mixed (split-plot) effects when supported between-subject factors or panels are present; within-subject rows use the Greenhouse-Geisser correction when needed.
  • Box / violin / raincloud show each occasion's full distribution.
  • The Y-axis title starts empty on purpose, as a nudge to name your measure (the Check graph tab reminds you until you do).

Scatter

Two numeric variables · long data · one row per point

The relationship between two continuous variables, with optional fit lines, confidence bands, correlation statistics, data ellipses, density contours, rug marks, and marginal distributions.

Variable boxTakesRequired?
X Variable / Y Variablenumeric variablesYes
Group By (optional)categorical → colors points and splits fit lines per groupNo
Panels (optional)categorical → one mini-chart per levelNo
Size By (bubble, optional)numeric → sizes each point (bubble chart)No
Label Points By (optional)any ID / name column → a small text label beside each pointNo
Scatter

One dot per observation; look for trend, spread, and outliers.

Fit line + band

Added from the "+" menu: linear, polynomial, or LOESS, with a confidence band.

Heatmap

For big samples: bins the plot into cells and colors by count, so dense areas stay readable.

Bubbles + labels

Size By turns points into bubbles; Label Points By tags each point.

Special features

  • "+" menu overlays: Fit line, Confidence band, Statistics box (r, p, n), Outliers, Data ellipses, Density contours, Rug marks, and marginal Distributions.
  • Correlation statistics per group and panel (Pearson, Spearman, and Kendall) in the Σ panel, with an on-chart r/p/n overlay.
  • Built for size: beyond a few thousand points the chart switches to a fast rendering layer automatically, so 20,000 points stay smooth. Overplotting is flagged by Check graph, with a one-click "Switch to Heatmap".

Distribution

One numeric variable · long data

Examine the distribution of a single numeric variable, optionally split by group or panel.

Variable boxTakesRequired?
Variableone numeric variableYes
Group By (optional)categorical → overlaid / side-by-side distributionsNo
Panels (optional)categorical → one mini-chart per levelNo
Histogram (default)

The shape of the distribution via binned counts. Bins, statistic (Count / Density / Proportion), and stacking are all editable on-chart.

Density

A smoothed histogram; the area under each curve is 1, so different-sized groups compare fairly.

Histogram + Density

Binned counts with a smooth curve drawn over them.

Q-Q Plot

Are the data roughly normal? Points hug the line if so. Add the confidence band from the "+" menu.

ECDF

The cumulative proportion of the data at or below each value.

Box

Median, middle 50%, whiskers, outliers, split by group if you add one.

Violin

The full distribution shape.

Raincloud

Distribution plus every raw value.

Special features

  • "+" menu overlays: Rug marks (a tick per raw value along the axis) and a Normal curve (on histogram / density types); a Confidence band on the Q-Q plot.
  • Normal curve example:
  • Shapiro-Wilk normality tests per cell, with plain-language verdicts, in the Σ panel's Normality tab, plus a one-click "Switch to the Q-Q plot".
  • The classic frequency table (class intervals, counts, %, cumulative %) in the Σ panel on histogram types; it is sortable and matches the chart's exact bins.
  • Stacked histograms: with a Group By variable you can dodge or stack the bins; drag a stacked segment up or down to reorder the stacking.
  • Statistic hops (Count ↔ Density ↔ Proportion), bin count, and bandwidth all update the chart instantly.

Frequencies

One categorical variable · long data · the module counts the rows

Variable boxTakesRequired?
Variableone categorical variable (the categories to count)Yes
Group By (optional)categorical → side-by-side / stacked breakdownNo
Panels (optional)categorical → one mini-chart per levelNo

On-chart controls: on a bar chart, the Display control keeps two related choices together: Bar height (Count / Percent / Proportion) and, when Group By is present, Group layout (Side by side / Stacked / Stacked 100%). The Σ Statistics panel contains the pairwise proportion tests and their p-value corrections.

Bar (default)

Counts or percentages per category: side-by-side, stacked, or 100% stacked.

Stacked 100% + Panels

Compare the response mix across categories and panels.

Pie

Parts of a whole. Best with about 6 slices or fewer.

Donut

A pie with the centre open. Drag the inner edge to resize the hole.

Pareto

Counts sorted largest-first with a cumulative % line, to find the few categories that dominate.

Special features

  • Chi-square tests: the Σ panel's Chi-square tab reports χ², df, N, p, effect size, expected-count checks, and the biggest departures through standardized residuals. Chi-square results deliberately stay in Statistics rather than adding a plot or readout plate to the chart.
  • Proportion brackets: on the side-by-side bar chart, significance brackets run two-proportion z-tests (the same math as jamovi's own tests).
  • Pie / donut gestures: drag a slice body to reorder slices; drag the seam between two slices to rotate the whole pie (hold Shift for 15° steps); drag a donut's inner edge to resize the hole. Tiny slices get leader-line callout labels automatically.
  • Pooling is never silent: pie, donut, and pareto count only the main Variable; if a Group By or Panels variable is assigned, a small "Pooled across …" note appears on the chart (Pareto does use Panels: one ranked mini-chart per panel).
  • Value labels print the count (or % in percent modes) on each bar or segment.
Honest by designHiding a bar on a Frequencies chart only declutters the picture: the chi-square, the brackets, and every table still include the hidden data: hiding never changes an N, a percent, or a p-value here. (Compare Groups and Repeated Measures use the opposite rule; see The hidden-data rule.)

Correlation Matrix

Two or more numeric variables

Variable boxTakesRequired?
Variablestwo or more numeric variablesYes (≥ 2)

On-chart controls: choose Pearson, Spearman, or Kendall from the chart's correlation controls. Each pair reports the correlation, p, and n using pairwise-complete cases (missing values drop per pair, not per row).

Heatmap (default)

Every pairwise correlation as a colored cell on a diverging scale.

Circles

Area-true circles: bigger = stronger, color = direction.

Numbers

The exact r values, with optional significance stars and fading of non-significant cells.

Mixed

Numbers on the lower triangle, circles on the upper: both views at once.

Special features (click any cell to reach them; tabs Cells · Values · Significance · Layout · Order)

  • Layout control: full matrix or lower / upper triangle; diagonal as 1s or blank.
  • Significance display: stars, and non-significant cells shown / faded / blanked / crossed out, at your chosen level.
  • Matrix-wide p adjustment: Bonferroni, Holm, or FDR across all pairs at once; every star, fade, tooltip, and panel number follows it.
  • Drag to reorder: grab any cell and drag vertically to move its row variable, horizontally to move its column variable; labels and the mirrored triangle glide along.
  • The color scale legend is draggable and clickable (orientation, ticks, title, size).
  • Cells that cannot be computed (too few complete pairs, no variation) draw as an em-dash rather than pretending.

Likert / Survey

A battery of items sharing one response scale · wide data · one column per item

Your data (wide)
EnjoyClassFairGradingWorkload
AgreeNeutralDisagree
Strongly AgreeAgreeNeutral
………
Variable boxTakesRequired?
Itemstwo or more items sharing one response scale; factor levels ("Strongly Disagree" … "Strongly Agree") and numeric codings (1-5, 0-10) both workYes
Diverging Stacked (default)

Items centred on the middle: disagreement left, agreement right. Best for the balance of opinion.

Stacked 100%

Full-width bars split by response, to compare the response mix.

Item Means

Each item's average with a confidence interval.

Special features

  • Reverse-scoring: click an item's (question) label and tick "Reverse-score this item" → the item is reflected (mean becomes (k+1) − mean, percentages mirror) and its label gains an "(R)" suffix everywhere. Cronbach's alpha reflects it too.
  • Sorting: by net agreement (descending / ascending), or drag rows into any custom order.
  • Cronbach's alpha in the Σ panel's Reliability tab, with an honest footnote about what internal consistency does and does not mean.
  • The response-scale legend is a first-class object: click it for size / color / orientation controls, drag it anywhere.
  • Numeric batteries: 0-10 ratings typed as continuous plot exactly like factor items. If items have more than 25 distinct values (true continuous measurements), stacking is impossible, so the module switches itself to a means-only chart and says so. A many-level text battery is refused with an explanation (those belong in Frequencies).

Around the chart: the toolbar

Every chart draws a button strip above the plot. Left to right:

ButtonNameWhat it does
"Bar ▾" (current type + icon)Chart typeOpens the chart-type flyout: a tile grid of every type this analysis offers. Click a tile to switch instantly; styling carries over.
Swatches + "Theme ▾"ThemeOpens two clearly separated tabs. Colors changes the graph's color palette and provides saved palettes plus a curated list: Plot Studio (the stock accessible default), Spectrum Dark II, Spectrum Muted IV (colorblind-safe), Okabe-Ito, Tableau 10 (qualitative), and Blues / Greens / Reds / Oranges / Purples / Greys / Cividis (sequential ramps that spread across your group count). Chart style applies a saved complete look, including any captured colors, fonts, axes, elements, and background. Footer links open the full palette gallery and styles manager. Hidden on Correlation and Likert charts, which use their own diverging scales.
↶ ↷Undo / RedoUndo or redo any on-chart edit (Cmd/Ctrl+Z, Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+Z). Everything is undoable, including a whole style or palette apply.
ΣStatisticsThe numbers behind the chart: descriptives, pairwise comparisons, ANOVAs, chi-square, normality, correlations, reliability, in per-module tabs. See Statistics.
👁Show / hide elementsThe Visibility panel: hide or restore anything (axes, legend, bars, groups, slices, overlays, annotations). The icon shows a count when things are hidden.
⚙Chart settingsChart-level options in five tabs: Appearance, Sizing, Palette (the full gallery + custom palette builder), Chart styles, and Accessibility (the Vision check).
⌕Find a settingA fuzzy, graph-aware search for the current module and graph type (Cmd/Ctrl+F). It understands nearby wording and statistical terms, then opens the matching panel, tab, or control without changing a value. A result for an unavailable overlay points to its existing Add-menu entry instead of adding it automatically.
+Add to chartOverlays and annotations: labels, brackets, reference lines, shapes, notes, rug marks, fit lines… See Adding things.
⭳Export plotSave the figure as SVG, PDF, PNG, or JPG. See Exporting.
?Help & shortcutsThe help family: Basics (tips, shortcuts, and the Accessibility topic), Label parts, Which graph?, Check graph, Glossary.
Search examplesIn Compare Groups, searching for "t test" opens Statistics → Compare pairs and "ANOVA" opens Omnibus. In Distribution, "Shapiro-Wilk" opens Normality; in Frequencies, "chi-square" opens Chi-square. These are navigation shortcuts, not commands: search never changes the selected test or runs a different analysis.

Hiding and restoring things

The eye button opens the Visibility panel, a tabbed list of everything hideable: a Hidden tab (everything currently hidden, one click restores each), axis tabs (line, ticks, tick labels, title), Legend, the per-module data tab (Bars / Groups / Slices / Variables / Items), Overlays, Other (error bars, data points, grid, value labels…), and Annotations.

You can also hide things directly: select any element and press Delete, or use the eye icon in its style panel's title bar. Everything comes back from the Visibility panel's Hidden tab.

TipHiding is also available per legend entry: Alt+click a legend entry to show that series alone; Alt+click again to bring the others back.

Undo covers everything

Every edit you make on the chart (colors, drags, reorders, hides, annotation moves, axis ranges, a whole palette pick or style apply, even the accessibility "Fix colors") is one Cmd/Ctrl+Z away, and repaints instantly. Undo history is kept locally on your computer, so it survives chart rebuilds and re-opening the widget. Two things are deliberately outside undo: switching the chart type (switch back from the type flyout instead) and one-shot actions like exporting a file or saving / renaming library palettes and styles.

Editing the appearance

Click-to-edit basics

  • Click any part of the chart (a bar, point, line, slice, axis, tick label, legend, title) and its style panel opens directly below the chart, titled after what you clicked (e.g. "Bars - F", "Y axis", "Legend").
  • Double-click any text (titles, category names, tick labels) to edit it in place.
  • Panels are organized into small tabs (the bar panel: Bars · Border · Error bars · Gap · Order) with compact control strips: Summary, Color, Pattern, Opacity, Corner, Width, Style…
  • Each panel's title bar has an eye (hide this element) and, for annotations, a trash button.
  • Esc steps back one layer at a time: color picker → panel → chart.

Style one series, or all of them

When a chart has several series, many style controls carry a small two-button scope toggle. The first button is named after what you clicked (the series' own name); the second reads "All bars" / "All slices" / "All lines" / "All levels" / "All curves" / "All fits", depending on the chart. The named side edits just that series; the "All …" side writes the chart-wide value and clears per-series overrides for that property so everything follows. The choice is remembered per control, so Pattern can stay per-series while Opacity stays chart-wide. Fill color is always per-series; that is what the palette is for.

Colors and palettes

  • Quick palette switch: toolbar Theme ▾ → Colors. Picking a palette recolors the whole chart instantly and clears per-series overrides; one Undo reverts.
  • The full gallery lives in Chart settings → Palette: 50+ palettes in Built-in / Colorblind-safe / Qualitative / Sequential / Diverging categories. Ramp palettes (Blues, Viridis…) automatically spread across however many groups your chart has: three groups on "Blues" get dark / mid / light, not three near-identical blues. A CB badge marks palettes whose colors stay separable under color-blindness at your current group count.
  • Build your own: the gallery's Build your own editor lets you click swatches to edit them, drag to reorder, add up to 12 colors, and lock the ones you want kept; Suggest colors proposes a fresh set around your locks (with style strategies like "Similar shades" and "Spread out", or Surprise). Tweak these colors → turns any existing palette into your own editable copy. Save… stores it in your library; Use as default for new charts makes every new analysis start with it.
  • Per-series colors: click the element (bar, point series, slice, segment) and pick a color. The picker docks on the panel's right side with two tabs: HSV (saturation / value square, hue and lightness strips, a hex field, and an eyedropper "Pick color from screen" where the browser supports it) and Swatches (your recent colors, the stock quick row, and a full hue grid). A checkerboard "transparent" chip appears where "no fill" makes sense.
  • "Follow … colors" toggles appear on overlays (error bars, fit lines, connectors, rug marks, normal curves…). The label names the source, such as Follow graph colors, Follow point colors, or Follow line color. When the toggle is on, the overlay inherits that color automatically. Picking a custom color turns following off; switching it back on restores the live link.

The Vision check (colorblind-safe charts)

Chart settings → Accessibility shows the Vision check, with five tiles: Normal, Protan. (red-blind), Deutan. (green-blind), Tritan. (blue-blind), and No color (grayscale). Click a tile to preview the whole chart as that viewer would see it. The preview is session-only and marked by a small amber badge on the chart ("click to turn off the preview"); the dot on each tile shows whether your current series colors pass the separation check there.

  • If something fails, a status line names exactly which vision types are affected, and a Fix colors button re-spreads your colors' lightness while keeping each series' hue (the red one stays red), changing as few colors as possible. Try another arrangement cycles alternatives; Revert restores everything.
  • Lock any series' swatch to protect it ("keep my brand red, move everything else"); the button becomes "Fix unlocked colors".
  • Adjust a color opens the normal picker per series, with the pass / warn dots updating live as you drag.
  • Make these my default palette saves the settled colors as "My accessible palette" and starts every new chart with it.
TipThe stock Plot Studio palette already passes every vision check by construction, so you will usually only meet the fixer after building a custom palette. Pattern fills (bar panel → Pattern) are a second cue that survives grayscale printing.

Chart styles: save a whole look

A chart style captures up to five parts of a chart's look (colors for the palette, text for font and sizes, axes, bars for corner / borders / patterns / markers, and background) so you can restyle any chart in two clicks and keep every figure in a paper consistent.

  • Save: Chart settings → Chart styles → save the current chart's look under a name, choosing which of the five parts to capture.
  • Apply: the fastest route is toolbar Theme ▾ → Chart style. Click a saved style to apply the whole look (one Undo reverts). The Chart settings styles tab also offers Apply parts… to apply only some groups.
  • Default for new charts: star a style ("Set as default") and every brand-new analysis starts with it. Existing charts are never restyled behind your back.
  • Each style card has a menu: Apply / Apply parts… / Update from this chart / Rename / Set as default / Delete.
  • In the panel's own words: "A style never touches your variables, graph type, statistics, error-bar type, legend position, axis ranges, or annotations. Apply is one Undo away (Cmd/Ctrl+Z)."

Text, titles, and fonts

  • Click any text to open its text panel: a rename box (where renaming makes sense, with a "Reset" back to the automatic text), size with B/I, color, and rotation where applicable. Computed labels (value labels, slice percentages, printed r values) offer styling but no rename; their text is the data.
  • Edit in place: double-click any text to type directly on the chart: Enter commits, Shift+Enter makes a new line, Esc cancels. Text elements can also be dragged to reposition, with alignment guides.
  • Chart title and axis titles: hover just above the chart / beside an axis and click the "Click to add title" zone; or rename existing ones by clicking them. The Figure note ("+" menu) adds an APA-style "Note." caption below the chart.
  • Category and item renames (X categories, correlation variables, Likert items) live in the same text panels and persist with the analysis; your data columns are never touched.
  • Chart-wide looks live in Chart settings → Appearance: Font family (Sans-serif / Serif / Monospace / Helvetica / Times / Courier), base Text color, Background (including Transparent), chart Border (None / Thin / Thick), and Orientation (Vertical / Horizontal, on the chart families where flipping makes sense).

Axes, gridlines, and layout

  • Click an axis for its panel: line color / width / style, tick marks (length, direction, minor ticks), and the Range strip (Min / Max / Step boxes with an "auto" reset). A checkbox "Show axis break (//) when min ≠ 0" keeps a truncated axis honest (Check graph warns if you cut an axis and switch it off). On Scatter, the Range strip adds a Scale switch (Linear / Log₁₀). You can also drag directly on the axis to adjust the range.
  • Gridlines: add from the "+" menu if hidden; click a gridline for the Grid panel (Major / Minor tabs, each with color / width / style plus direction and layer); hovering highlights the line you are about to click.
  • Reorder categories or series by dragging them on the chart, or from the Order tab of the element's panel with the compact up / down buttons. Order controls move one item at a time; they do not re-sort categories by their values.
  • Resize the plot by dragging the grips at the chart's edges (hold Shift to keep proportions), or set exact dimensions in Chart settings → Sizing.
  • Panels (facets): each panel heading is clickable and stylable. If you switch panels to independent Y scales, the Check graph tab will expect a figure-note disclosure, because equal heights stop meaning equal values.
  • Legend: click it for swatch size, spacing, and gap; drag the whole legend anywhere, or drag individual rows to rearrange them (a "Reset to auto" button undoes a custom layout). Hover zones offer "Click to add legend title".

Adding things: the "+" menu

The toolbar + button ("Add to chart") lists exactly what fits the current chart type:

ItemAppears onWhat it adds
Text annotationevery chartA free text label; drag anywhere, style freely.
Figure noteevery chartAn APA-style "Note." caption below the plot (part of the exported figure).
Sig. bracketbar / line / dot / box / violin / raincloud (Compare Groups, Repeated Measures; Frequencies' side-by-side bar)A significance bracket that computes a real test; see below.
Reference linecharts with value axesA draggable horizontal or vertical line at an exact value.
Draw shapes…every chartA drawing mode: lines, arrows, rectangles, ellipses, and more.
Grid linesaxis chartsTurns gridlines on and opens their styling panel.
Outliersbar / line / dot summary chartsMarks per-cell outliers.
Data pointsbar / line / dot / box / violinOverlays every raw observation.
Connect subjectsRepeated Measures, with data points onPer-subject "spaghetti" lines across occasions.
Value labels / N labelsbars (+ pareto); N labels also on box / violin / raincloudPrints the value / the cell's n on each bar.
Slice labelspie / donutRe-enables slice percentage labels.
Rug marks / Normal curve / Confidence bandDistribution typesDistribution overlays (band = Q-Q plot).
Fit line / Confidence band / Statistics box / Data ellipses / Density contours / Rug marks / MarginalsScatterThe regression and point-distribution overlay families.

Most items follow an enable-then-open pattern: the layer switches on and its styling panel opens, so you can restyle or remove it (trash / eye) right away.

Reference lines

  • Drag the line to move it; the position readout follows live. Drag its end handles to shorten or extend the span.
  • Or type an exact value: the panel's Position strip has a "Y value =" (or "X value =") box calibrated to the axis; typing 50 puts the line exactly at 50.
  • Style strips: Color, Width, Style (Solid / Dashed / Long dash / Dotted), Orientation (horizontal / vertical), Layer (behind / in front of the data).

Drawing shapes

  • "+" → Draw shapes… opens a picker with nine kinds: Line, Arrow, Curve, Rectangle, Ellipse, Triangle, Polygon, Diamond, Star. Pick one, then drag on the chart to draw it, or just click to drop a default-size shape.
  • Hold Shift while drawing for straight / square shapes. Drawing snaps to bars, markers, axis lines, and ticks.
  • Each new shape opens its editor immediately: resize handles, a rotate handle (Shift snaps to 15° steps), and a bend handle on curves, plus color, width, and style controls. Shift while resizing locks the aspect ratio (or 45° angles on lines and arrows).
  • Alt-drag a shape to drag away a copy; Cmd/Ctrl+D duplicates; Delete deletes a drawn shape outright (undo restores it).
  • Esc or the Done button exits drawing mode.

Statistics

The Σ Statistics panel

The toolbar Σ button opens the numbers behind the chart, with tabs per analysis:

AnalysisTabsHighlights
Compare Groups / Repeated MeasuresCompare pairs · Omnibus · DescriptivesEvery pairwise test with corrections and one-click bracket placement; supported one- to three-factor ANOVA effects, including repeated-measures and mixed (split-plot) effects with Greenhouse-Geisser correction on within-subject rows when needed; full descriptives.
FrequenciesChi-square · Pairwise · Countsχ² with plain-language "biggest departure" lines; Holm-adjusted pairwise proportions; raw counts with per-panel percents.
DistributionNormality · Descriptives · Frequency tableShapiro-Wilk per cell with careful verdict copy; skew / kurtosis; the classic sortable class-interval table (histogram types).
ScatterCorrelation + fit (per cell)Pearson r with a Fisher-z CI, Spearman ρ (Kendall τ when selected), linear slope and R², per group × panel.
Correlation MatrixMatrix summary · All pairsStrongest pair, significant-pair tally, copyable r matrix, every pair listed once.
Likert / SurveyItem means · ReliabilityMeans ± CI with per-level percents; Cronbach's alpha with its caveat.

Panel-wide behaviors:

  • Linked to the chart, both ways. Hover a table row → a dashed ring previews the matching bars on the chart. Click the row → the ring pins with a marching-ants animation; hover a bar → every row mentioning it highlights. Click panel whitespace to release the pin.
  • The focus card. Pinning a row shows a reading card above the table: section context, the row's APA sentence (or its values), a Copy button, and ‹ › steppers that walk the pin row by row while the chart rings follow.
  • Sticky stats mode. While the panel is open, plain chart clicks drive the panel instead of opening style editors: click a bar to see its numbers, click two bars (Compare pairs tab) to pin their test. Click open space to clear; the Σ button or Esc exits. With the panel closed, clicks are ordinary click-to-edit again.
  • Cmd/Ctrl+click any bar, from anywhere, to jump straight into the panel with that cell's numbers pinned.
  • Tap-a-term definitions. Column headers and many value cells are tappable: a popover gives a plain-language definition plus an "In this table" reading computed from that row's own numbers ("Here t(11.7) = 2.77 for A vs B: the two group means sit about 2.77 standard errors apart…"). Esc closes it.
  • Copy everywhere: per-row APA sentences, Copy APA on omnibus cards, and Copy table buttons that paste straight into Excel or Word.
  • Alpha is a reading lens (.10 / .05 / .01 / .001): it recolors the green significance chips and tallies without changing anything saved.

Compare pairs → Place brackets

The Compare pairs tab (Compare Groups / Repeated Measures) enumerates every within-panel pair of cells through the same test engine the brackets use:

  1. Compare (grouped charts): "Both (recommended)" lists simple effects in both directions and drops hard-to-interpret diagonal pairs (disclosed in the footnote; "Every pair" restores them).
  2. Test: Auto (recommended) / Welch's t / Student's t / Mann-Whitney U, plus Paired t and Wilcoxon signed-rank on Repeated Measures.
  3. Correct: None / Bonferroni / Holm / Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR), plus Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett (vs control) on Compare Groups. The chosen correction is chart-wide, shared with on-chart brackets.
  4. Tick the rows you want on the figure, then click Place brackets: brackets are drawn collision-free, tiered above the data, expanding the axis if needed. The ▾ half of the button picks the label style (Asterisks, p value, p + effect size, or Full APA) and re-labels already-placed brackets live. Clear placed removes only the auto-placed set; hand-made brackets are never touched.

Rows are grouped into sections ("Within Ctrl", "Across x", "Mixed pairs") with per-section significant counts; section headers fold; sorting by chart order or by p is one click on the column headers.

Significance brackets

Add one from the "+" menu, then drag each bracket leg onto a bar to anchor which two cells it compares. The bracket's Stats tab computes a real test from your raw data:

  • Test: Auto (recommended) resolves to a paired test for two occasions of the same subjects and Welch's otherwise; or pick Welch's t, Student's t, Paired t, Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon signed-rank, or an omnibus: One-way ANOVA (X-axis or grouping factor), or One-way RM-ANOVA (occasions) on Repeated Measures. Frequencies bars use a Proportion z-test.
  • Label: Asterisks / p-value / p + effect size / full APA. Effect: Cohen's d or Hedges' g (the right effect size is forced for rank, paired, ANOVA, and proportion tests). Tails: two- or one-tailed.
  • Correct: the chart-wide multiple-comparison correction (None / Bonferroni / Holm / FDR / Tukey / Games-Howell / Dunnett). A live monospace readout shows the test, statistic, raw and adjusted p, CI, effect size, and exactly what the label will say; Copy APA copies the sentence.
  • Honesty built in: if a correction cannot apply to a bracket's test (e.g. Tukey on repeated measures), the raw p is shown with a disclosure line saying so. Tests that do not fit the data are refused with an explanation in plain language, never computed wrong.

On-chart statistical displays

The Statistics panel keeps inferential results in the lower panel unless a chart feature explicitly belongs on the figure. Scatter's + → Statistics box adds a draggable r / p / n readout that updates with the chart. Significance brackets are added separately. Frequencies keeps chi-square results in Σ Statistics only; there is no chi-square plot or on-chart χ² plate.

The hidden-data rule

One rule, stated everywhere

On measurement charts (Compare Groups, Repeated Measures), hiding is excluding. Hide a bar or points and the brackets and ANOVA recompute without them, matching the drawn error bars, which already do. The readouts disclose it.

On count charts (Frequencies) and in every table, hiding is decluttering. The chi-square, proportion tests, and all tables keep the full data: hiding a bar never silently changes an N, a percent, or a p.

The Σ panel, the bracket readouts, and the Check graph tab all state which side of the rule your chart is on whenever anything is hidden.

Teaching & checking tools (the "?" family)

The ? button opens a five-tab help family: Basics · Label parts · Which graph? · Check graph · Glossary. Inside Basics, topic pills split the tips by task: Start here · Arrange · Annotate · Accessibility · All shortcuts. Basics also carries an Open the user guide button that opens this very guide in your browser.

Label parts: name every part of the chart

The Label parts tab overlays named callout chips on every part of the live chart (bars, error bars, axes, ticks, category labels, the legend, brackets, curves), each with a leader line pointing at it.

  • Hover a chip for an explanation that reflects your actual settings (the Error bar chip says whether yours show SE, SD, or a CI).
  • Click a part or its chip to read its description in the panel below; "Open this part's editor" jumps straight to styling it.
  • Drag chips anywhere; click a different bar or tick to move a chip to that one.
  • The labels stay up while you work. Esc or Clear Labels dismisses them.

Check graph: a pitfall scanner

The Check graph tab runs your chart against the full rubric of classic graphing pitfalls for its chart type. The header says how many checks ran; warnings and tips appear as cards (many with a one-click fix, e.g. "Switch to Heatmap"), and everything that passed shows as green pills you can hover to see what was checked.

The full list of checks (40)

WARN= likely misleading, fix before sharing. TIP= worth a look.

WARNZero baselineBars and histogram columns are read by height, so the value axis should start at 0. Fires when the Y minimum is raised above 0 on bar-family charts.
WARNAxis cut is markedOn position-encoded charts (line, dot, box…), starting above zero is fine if the axis break mark (//) stays on. Fires when the min is raised and the break mark is off.
TIPAxis cut disclosedWhen an above-zero minimum is paired with the axis break mark, the cut is visible and legitimate; this informational note asks you to confirm that the tighter window is intentional.
WARNAxis range shows all dataAxis limits should not push bars, boxes, or points outside the plot window.
WARNEnough data for the shapeBox / violin shapes need about n ≥ 10 per group; offers "Switch to Raincloud".
TIPViolin sample sizeA violin's smooth outline is trustworthy from about n = 20; offers "Switch to Box plot".
TIPUncertainty shownSummary charts should show error bars (SE, SD, or a CI).
TIPComparable group sizesCells with very different ns should not look interchangeable (max/min ≥ 5 fires it); suggests N labels.
TIPMean fits the dataA strongly skewed cell is poorly summarized by its mean; names the worst cell, offers raincloud / box.
WARNBracket test matches the stated estimandA median-centered chart can carry a mean test only when the caption makes that deliberate choice clear; rank tests address rank distributions, not medians themselves.
WARNError bars visibleMarkers should not cover their own error bars.
TIPOrdered X for a lineA line implies the X axis has an order; offers "Switch to Bar".
TIPReadable group countPast about 7–8 colored groups, colors become harder to tell apart.
WARNReadable slice countPies read best with ≤ 6 slices; offers "Switch to Pareto".
TIPEnough data per panelEvery facet panel needs enough observations to show more than noise (smallest panel n < 10 fires).
TIPFree Y scales disclosedIndependent panel Y ranges must be named in the figure note.
TIPOverplotting> 500 scatter points: offers "Switch to Heatmap".
TIPEnough points for a fit lineA regression line on fewer than ~10 points is fragile.
TIPLog axis flaggedA log axis should say "log" in its title.
TIPSample size for a Q-Q plotn < 20: wander off the line is expected; points at the confidence band.
TIPSample size for a density curven < 20: the bumps are mostly smoothing; offers "Switch to Histogram".
TIPBin count fits the dataFar more bins than values reads as noise.
TIPEnough points for a heatmapA heatmap with < 100 points suits a scatter better; one-click back.
WARNChi-square expected countsThe approximation needs expected counts ≥ ~5 per cell.
WARNPairs behind each correlationCorrelations on < 10 complete pairs swing wildly.
TIPMany-tests caveat≥ 10 correlations tested with significance marks and no adjustment.
TIPMultiple-comparisons handling≥ 3 significance brackets call for a correction or a stated caveat.
WARNResponses per itemLikert items answered by < 10 people have unstable percentages.
TIPMeans on a short scaleAveraging a 2-4-point scale hides the response mix; offers the diverging view.
WARNValue-axis title / X-axis titleAxes should say what was measured, in what units.
WARNValue-axis numbers / X-axis labelsTick numbers and category labels must be visible.
WARNColors have a keyWhen color carries meaning, a legend or direct labels must decode it.
TIPNo hidden dataParts hidden with the eye tool should be shown or disclosed in the figure note; states the module's hidden-data rule.
WARNDistinguishable colorsTwo series' colors are identical or nearly so; lists the pairs.
TIPColorblind safetyColor pairs are re-tested under simulated red-green color blindness; suggests the colorblind-safe palettes or patterns.
TIPError bars explainedIf you wrote a figure note, it should say whether error bars are SE, SD, or a CI (a chart with no note is never nagged).
TIPP-value correction in the noteIf a correction adjusted a displayed bracket, the note should name it.
TIPAsterisk key in the noteStar-labelled brackets should carry the key "* p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001."
Footer, quoted"These are general heuristics, not strict rules; your design may have good reasons to differ."

Glossary

The Glossary tab holds 131 plain-language entries in 14 topic groups. Along with significance, effect sizes, tests, ANOVA, corrections, descriptives, frequencies, correlation, and reliability, it now covers smoothing and density curves; scatter fits and overlays; Q-Q, ECDF, and specialty charts; raw data points; axes, panels, and layout; and Likert charts. Every entry has a definition, a "How to read it" box, and (the teaching heart of it) a "Common misread" box. The search is typo-tolerant: "anovva" still finds ANOVA, and you can search by the idea ("spread", "difference") rather than the term.

The same dictionary powers the Σ panel's tap-a-term popovers, so a student can tap "p (Holm)" in a live table and get the definition plus a sentence computed from that row's own numbers.

Keyboard & mouse reference

(⌘ on Mac; Ctrl on Windows / Linux. This is the same table as the chart's ? → Basics → All shortcuts.)

Mouse

GestureDoes
Click a chart partOpens its style panel.
Double-click textEdit it in place.
Drag bars / segments / slices / rows / cells / legendReorder them. Grouped bars: dragging one reorders its series; hold Shift to move the whole category.
Drag a pie seam / donut inner edgeRotate the pie (Shift = 15° steps) / resize the hole.
Alt+click a legend entryShow that series alone; again to restore.
⌘/Ctrl+click a bar or markerOpen the Statistics panel with its numbers; with the panel open, plain clicks keep driving it.
Drag annotations / brackets / reference linesMove them. Alt-drag = drag away a copy; Shift = keep the move perfectly horizontal / vertical.
Drag the chart-edge gripsResize the plot (Shift keeps proportions).
Drag on empty spaceMarquee-select every annotation and text label inside the box (Shift adds to the selection), then move, nudge, hide, or duplicate them together. ⌘/Ctrl+click also adds or removes individual text elements from a multi-selection.
Double-click empty backgroundOpens Chart settings (background, border, fonts).
Right-click an individual data pointA small menu with one action: "Hide this point" (the only right-click menu; restore from the eye button).
HoverTooltips with the exact values (bars, points, slices, matrix cells); hovered lines and ticks thicken so you know what you are about to click.

Keyboard

KeysDoes
DeleteHide the selected element (restore via the eye button). A selected drawn shape is deleted instead; undo brings it back.
⌘+Z / ⌘+Shift+ZUndo / redo styling changes.
⌘+C / ⌘+VCopy & paste annotations.
⌘+DDuplicate the selected annotation.
⌘+Shift+C / ⌘+Shift+VCopy a bar or slice's style, then paste it onto another.
Alt+←↑↓→Nudge the selected annotation 1 px (Alt+Shift = 10 px).
⌘+←↑↓→Move the selected bar, slice, item, or series one step in its order.
⌘+ASelect every annotation (then nudge, hide, or duplicate them together).
⌘/Ctrl+FOpen Find a setting; type a control, concept, or statistical term, then use the arrow keys and Enter to open the result.
?Open the help panel.
Tab / Shift+TabMove between the toolbar, the chart, and an open editor's controls.
←→ on the chartStep between sibling parts (bar to bar, slice to slice); a dashed outline marks the current one and a screen reader announces it.
↑↓ or Tab on the chartStep between kinds of part: bars, axes, legend…
EnterOpen the selected part's editor and jump into its controls.
EscStep back one layer: color picker → editor → chart.
AccessibilityThe whole chart is keyboard-navigable and self-describing: it announces its type, axes, categories, and groups to screen readers, and changes are announced as they happen. The ? → Basics → Accessibility topic collects all of this.

Exporting & sharing

The Export panel

  • Format: SVG, PDF, PNG, JPG. (SVG and PDF are vector, so they stay sharp at any size; use them for papers. PNG / JPG add a DPI choice: Screen (96) / 150 / Print (300) / 600 / Hi-res (1200).)
  • Filename and Save to (Downloads / Desktop / Documents) are remembered. Files are never overwritten; a duplicate name gets " (2)", " (3)"…
  • After Save, the result appears in the small Export Status item under the chart: "Saved <filename> to <folder>".
PDF notePDF export uses the R package rsvg. If it is missing, the status line says so: install it in jamovi's R with install.packages('rsvg'), or export SVG instead.

What travels with a .omv file, and what does not

  • The chart travels. Everything you set on a chart (colors, styles applied, annotations, titles, brackets) is stored in the analysis inside the .omv. A colleague who opens your file sees the chart exactly as you styled it.
  • Your library does not. Saved palettes, saved chart styles, and your defaults live on your machine (they follow you across all your files). Opening someone else's file can never add to or overwrite your library. To hand a look to a colleague, have them save the received chart's style on their machine ("Update from this chart" / save under a name).
  • Re-opening a shared file never silently writes exports to disk or re-saves palettes; those actions only fire when you click them.

Troubleshooting & FAQ

The chart area shows instructions instead of a chart

A required variable box is still empty; the placeholder tells you exactly which ("Drag a categorical variable into X-Axis Variable and a numeric variable into Y-Axis Variable to render…"). Fill the named boxes and the chart draws immediately. If a variable is assigned but every value is missing, the message says so ("<variable> has no usable (non-missing) values to plot.").

A small pill says "N of M cases not shown (missing values)"

Rows with missing values on the variables the chart uses were dropped (or partially used). The pill is informational and dismissible, and it is not part of the exported figure; if the exclusion matters for readers, state it in the Figure note. Each module counts in the role-appropriate way: Repeated Measures keeps subjects at the occasions where they were measured; Correlation uses pairwise-complete cases; Likert keeps each item's own n; Scatter ignores a missing Group By value (the point still plots, ungrouped).

My bars have no error bars

Three by-design reasons: the cell has fewer than 2 observations; Error Bars is set to None; or Summary is set to Median: SE, SD, and CI formulas describe the mean, so no bar is drawn around a median (the Σ Descriptives footnote explains). Box / violin / raincloud never draw summary error bars; the shape itself carries the spread.

I hid something and want it back

Toolbar eye button → the Hidden tab lists everything currently hidden; one click restores each. The eye icon itself shows a count while anything is hidden.

Likert refuses my items, or only offers "Means"

Items must share one small response scale. If your (all-numeric) items have more than 25 distinct values they are continuous measurements, so the module switches to the means-only chart and says so. If a many-level item is text, nothing sensible can be drawn and the module explains why (many-level categorical variables belong in Frequencies).

A statistic refuses to compute

That is deliberate: Plot Studio never computes a test that does not fit the data. The readout explains in plain language: for example, paired tests need matched within-subject data, rank and paired tests do not support every multiple-comparison correction, mixed ANOVA needs enough complete subjects in every design cell, and correlations need 3+ complete pairs. The wording points to the relevant requirement or alternative.

The chart says "Loading chart engine…"

The chart engine's local cache was unavailable for one render. It self-heals on the next run of the analysis (change anything, or re-run). Your options and data are unaffected.

Where do my saved palettes and styles live?

In two small files (palettes.json, styles.json) in your personal Plot Studio configuration folder on your computer, which is why they are available in every jamovi file you open, and why they do not travel inside a shared .omv.

Something looks stuck after an edit

jamovi recomputes in the background after each edit; heavy charts can take a beat. Panels and menus are built to survive this, but if the display ever looks wrong, any small change (or Cmd/Ctrl+Z then redo) forces a clean redraw. If the whole module misbehaves after installation or reinstallation, restart jamovi; modules load at startup.

Plot Studio 2.9.0 · by Torry Scott Dennis, PhD · This guide describes the 2.9.0 release. The chart and feature-panel screenshots were generated from the current module.
